title: The Communication Process & Barriers
simple_html:
Communication is how meaning travels from one mind to another. It sounds simple, but a surprising amount gets lost along the way. Understanding the steps helps you spot exactly where things break down.

The basic idea

Think of communication as a chain. A sender has an idea, turns it into words or gestures (this is called encoding), and sends it through a channel (a face-to-face chat, an email, a phone call). The receiver takes in the message and interprets it (decoding). Then they send feedback so the sender knows whether the message landed.

Why it matters

Every step can introduce noise — anything that distorts the message. A noisy room, jargon the listener does not know, a distracted reader, or a defensive mood can all twist what was meant into something different from what was understood.

Key takeaways

  • Meaning is in people, not words. The same sentence can mean different things to different people based on their background.
  • Channels differ in richness. A live conversation carries tone and body language; a text message carries almost none, so it is easier to misread.
  • Feedback closes the loop. Without it, you are guessing whether you were understood. Asking "What did you take from that?" beats assuming.
  • Barriers are everywhere: physical (noise), semantic (unclear words), psychological (emotions, bias), and cultural (different norms).
  • You can fix most problems by simplifying your message, choosing the right channel, and confirming understanding.

Master this and every later skill — listening, persuasion, negotiation — becomes easier, because they all run on top of this same process.
intermediate_html:
The communication process is the foundational model for everything in this course. If you can diagnose where a message breaks down, you can fix almost any communication problem — and negotiation is, at its core, high-stakes communication.

The transmission model

The classic model has eight elements: sender → encoding → message → channel → decoding → receiver → feedback, with noise able to interfere at any point. The sender forms an intention and encodes it into symbols (language, tone, gestures). The message travels through a channel. The receiver decodes it into meaning, then provides feedback, which makes communication a two-way loop rather than a one-way broadcast.

Channel richness

Channels vary in their capacity to carry information. Channel richness ranks media by how much they convey: face-to-face is richest (words, tone, facial expression, instant feedback), followed by video, phone, then written forms like email and finally formal reports. The rule of thumb: match channel richness to message complexity and ambiguity. Use rich channels for sensitive, emotional, or ambiguous messages (firing someone, resolving conflict); use lean channels for simple, factual ones (a meeting time).

Four families of barriers

TypeExampleFix
PhysicalBackground noise, bad connectionChange environment or channel
SemanticJargon, ambiguous wordsUse plain language, define terms
PsychologicalEmotions, defensiveness, bias, filteringBuild rapport, manage emotion first
CulturalDifferent norms, high vs low contextAdapt style, confirm meaning

Worked example

A manager emails her team: "Let's circle back on the deck ASAP." One member assumes "ASAP" means today and stays late; another assumes "sometime this week"; a non-native speaker is unsure what "circle back" and "deck" mean. Here we see semantic noise (idioms, jargon) and a channel mismatch (a lean, ambiguous channel for a message needing coordination). The fix: a richer channel (a two-minute call), plain language ("Please send me the revised slides by 3 pm Thursday"), and a feedback request ("Reply to confirm the deadline works").

How to apply it

  • Encode for the receiver, not yourself. Anticipate their vocabulary, context, and likely emotional state.
  • Choose the channel deliberately. Ambiguous or emotional → rich channel. Routine → lean channel.
  • Reduce noise proactively: remove jargon, pick a quiet setting, and address strong emotions before content.
  • Always close the loop. Invite feedback and paraphrase back to confirm shared meaning.

This model connects directly to the next lessons: listening and questioning (Lesson 2) are the receiver-side and feedback skills, while persuasion (Lesson 3) is about high-quality encoding aimed at the receiver's interests.
full_html:
Communication is the substrate of all managerial and negotiation work. Before tactics, framing, or BATNA matter, two parties must successfully transfer meaning — and the central insight of communication theory is that this transfer is far more fragile and lossy than intuition suggests. This lesson builds the diagnostic model that the rest of the course relies on.

The Shannon-Weaver lineage

The dominant model derives from information theory (Shannon & Weaver, 1949), originally engineered to describe signal transmission over telephone lines. It frames communication as: an information source produces a message, a transmitter encodes it into a signal, the signal passes through a channel where noise may corrupt it, a receiver decodes it, and it reaches a destination. Schramm (1954) humanized this engineering model by adding two crucial ideas: fields of experience (a message is only shared insofar as sender and receiver overlap in vocabulary, culture, and frame of reference) and the feedback loop, which converts a one-way line into a circular, interactive process. Berlo's (1960) SMCR model (Source-Message-Channel-Receiver) further specified the human factors — communication skills, attitudes, knowledge, and social-cultural system — affecting each node.

The eight elements, precisely

  1. Sender / source — holds the communicative intention.
  2. Encoding — translating intention into transmittable symbols (verbal, paraverbal, nonverbal).
  3. Message — the encoded content actually sent (which may differ from what was intended).
  4. Channel — the medium of transmission.
  5. Receiver — the target who must decode.
  6. Decoding — reconstructing meaning from symbols.
  7. Feedback — the receiver's response, enabling correction.
  8. Noise — any interference distorting the signal.

The non-obvious lesson is that there are at least three distinct messages in play: what the sender intended, what was actually encoded and sent, and what was decoded. Misunderstanding is the gap between intention and decoding. As the cliche (often attributed to Shaw) warns, "the single biggest problem in communication is the illusion that it has taken place."

A taxonomy of noise / barriers

Following standard organizational-behavior treatments (e.g., Robbins & Judge), barriers cluster into:

  • Physical / mechanical noise: ambient sound, poor bandwidth, illegible print.
  • Semantic noise: jargon, ambiguity, connotation, and the fact that words are symbols whose meaning resides in people, not dictionaries.
  • Psychological noise: emotions, defensiveness, selective perception, and filtering (a sender deliberately manipulating information to be seen favorably — endemic in hierarchies).
  • Cultural noise: differing norms, especially Hall's (1976) high-context vs low-context distinction — high-context cultures embed meaning in setting and relationship; low-context cultures put meaning explicitly in words. (Developed in Lesson 4.)

Channel richness theory

Media richness theory (Daft & Lengel, 1986) explains why channel choice is a managerial competence, not a triviality. Richness is defined by four criteria: capacity for immediate feedback, number of cues carried (tone, gesture, posture), language variety (natural vs numeric), and personal focus. The ranking, richest to leanest: face-to-face > video > telephone > instant messaging > personal written (letter, email) > formal written (reports, bulletins) > numeric documents. The prescription: match richness to equivocality. For equivocal tasks (ambiguous, conflict-laden, novel), rich media reduce misunderstanding; for certain, routine tasks, lean media are efficient and create a useful record. Overusing rich media for simple messages wastes time; using lean media for emotional messages (the infamous layoff-by-text) breeds resentment and error.

Worked example with diagnosis

A VP wants to slow a stalled project. She forwards a complex spreadsheet with the note "thoughts?" The team reads it three ways: as a critique, a delegation, or a rhetorical aside. Diagnosis: (1) encoding failure — the intention ("I'm worried about the timeline; advise me") was compressed into one ambiguous word; (2) channel mismatch — an equivocal, emotionally tinged concern sent via a lean channel; (3) no feedback scaffold — "thoughts?" does not specify who, by when, or in what form. Remedy: a short synchronous meeting (rich channel) opening with the explicit concern, plain encoding, and a structured feedback request ("By Friday, each lead send me one risk and one mitigation").

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating words as containers of fixed meaning. Meaning is reconstructed by the receiver through their field of experience; the same word triggers different decodings.
  • Confusing transmission with understanding. Sending is not communicating; only confirmed decoding is.
  • Skipping feedback. Without the loop, errors compound silently. Paraphrasing ("so what you need from me is X") is the cheapest insurance available.
  • Defaulting to convenient channels. Email is convenient, not always appropriate; richness should be chosen against the message's ambiguity and emotional weight.
  • Ignoring noise sources you control, especially semantic noise (idioms with non-native speakers) and psychological noise (delivering content before emotions are settled).

Cross-links

This model is the backbone of the syllabus. Lesson 2 develops the receiver and feedback side — active listening, questioning, and decoding nonverbal cues. Lesson 3 treats persuasion as strategic, audience-centered encoding. Lesson 4 deepens cultural noise and high/low-context dynamics. And from Lesson 5 onward, every negotiation move is an instance of encoding interests, choosing channels, and managing the noise of emotion and bias.
